title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making
summary: Requires federal government institutions to assess and mitigate risks associated with automated decision systems before deploying them. Establishes impact levels (1–4) based on the significance of the automated decision, with progressively stricter requirements at higher levels including mandatory human review, algorithmic impact assessments, and public notice obligations.
scope: All federal government institutions using automated decision systems that render or support decisions affecting individuals or businesses.
key_provisions: Mandatory Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before deployment of any automated decision system
Four-tier impact classification: systems are rated Level 1–4 based on decision consequences (e.g., Level 4 = loss of liberty, life, or health)
Peer review and approval required for Level 3 and 4 systems
Plain-language notice to affected individuals whenever an automated system is used in a decision
Human review mechanism: affected parties must be able to request review by a qualified official
Explanation requirement: institutions must provide a meaningful explanation of how the system reached its recommendation
applies_to: Federal departments and agencies subject to the Financial Administration Act
Government institutions using systems that render or support administrative decisions
commencement_notes: Phased implementation: Level 1 systems in scope from April 1, 2019; Level 2 from April 1, 2020; Levels 3 and 4 from April 1, 2021. Significant amendments took effect March 31, 2023, strengthening notice and explanation requirements.

title: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
summary: Canada's primary private-sector privacy law. PIPEDA governs how organizations collect, use, and disclose personal information in the course of commercial activity. It applies directly to AI systems that process personal data, requiring meaningful consent, purpose limitation, accountability, and individual rights including access and correction. Organizations using AI for profiling or automated decisions involving personal information must comply.
scope: Private-sector organizations engaged in commercial activities across Canada (except in provinces with substantially similar legislation: Quebec, Alberta, British Columbia for provincially regulated activities).
key_provisions: Accountability: organizations must designate a Privacy Officer and implement data governance policies
Meaningful consent required before collecting, using, or disclosing personal information
Purpose limitation: data collected for one purpose cannot be used for an unrelated purpose without new consent
Individual rights: individuals may access their personal information and request correction
Breach notification: organizations must notify the Privacy Commissioner and affected individuals of significant data breaches
Adequacy: organizations transferring data internationally must ensure comparable protection
enforcement_body: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC)
penalty_range: Up to $100,000 per violation for certain offences under Part 1
applies_to: Private-sector organizations collecting, using, or disclosing personal information in commercial activities
AI systems that process personal data for profiling, automated decisions, or analytics
commencement_notes: PIPEDA has been in force since January 1, 2001, with phased extension to provinces. It is expected to be replaced or substantially amended by the Consumer Privacy Protection Act (CPPA) under Bill C-27, Part 1, once that bill is passed. As of April 2026, PIPEDA remains the applicable law as C-27 has not passed.

title: Act to Modernize Legislative Provisions Respecting the Protection of Personal Information (Law 25 / Bill 64)
summary: Comprehensive overhaul of Quebec's private-sector and public-sector privacy laws, introducing requirements closely aligned with the GDPR. Includes specific AI provisions: organizations must disclose when a decision is made solely through automated means and must, upon request, inform individuals of the personal information used and allow them to present their views to a human reviewer. Establishes a 72-hour breach notification requirement and mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) for high-risk projects.
scope: Organizations doing business in Quebec that collect, hold, use, or communicate personal information, including through automated decision-making systems.
key_provisions: Automated decision disclosure: individuals must be informed when a decision is based exclusively on automated processing of their personal information
Right to human review: individuals subject to solely automated decisions may request human intervention and present their views
Privacy by design: organizations must integrate privacy protections into projects involving personal information from the outset
Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) mandatory before any project likely to affect the privacy of individuals
72-hour breach notification to Commission d'accès à l'information (CAI) for confidentiality incidents presenting serious risk
Data minimization and purpose limitation requirements aligned with GDPR standards
Mandatory appointment of a Privacy Officer (Chief Privacy Officer) for all private-sector organizations
enforcement_body: Commission d'accès à l'information du Québec (CAI)
penalty_range: Up to $25,000,000 or 4% of worldwide turnover (whichever is greater) for most serious violations
applies_to: Private-sector enterprises doing business in Quebec
Quebec public bodies (under the Public Sector Act amendments)
AI systems making automated decisions using personal information about Quebec residents
commencement_notes: Phased implementation: First tranche (Sept 22, 2022) — privacy incident obligations, governance requirements. Second tranche (Sept 22, 2023) — automated decision disclosure, right to human review, PIAs, data minimization, breach notification obligations. Third tranche (Sept 22, 2024) — remaining provisions including data portability rights.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) — Bill C-27, Part 3
summary: Canada's first dedicated AI legislation, introduced as Part 3 of the Digital Charter Implementation Act (Bill C-27). AIDA would have required organizations developing or deploying high-impact AI systems to implement risk management frameworks, conduct impact assessments, and maintain transparency with affected individuals. It would also have created an AI and Data Commissioner. The bill died on the order paper when Parliament prorogued in January 2025.
scope: Would have applied to private-sector organizations designing, developing, or deploying high-impact AI systems in Canada.
key_provisions: Definition of 'high-impact AI system' based on significant potential harm to health, safety, economic interests, or fundamental rights
Mandatory risk assessment and mitigation measures for high-impact systems
Transparency obligations: individuals must be able to know when they interact with a high-impact AI system
Prohibition on AI systems causing serious harm or engaging in reckless deployment
Creation of an AI and Data Commissioner with audit and enforcement powers
Criminal penalties for most serious violations (e.g., using AI systems known to cause harm)
enforcement_body: Proposed AI and Data Commissioner (not yet established)
penalty_range: Up to $25,000,000 or 5% of global revenue for administrative violations; criminal penalties for most serious offences
applies_to: Private-sector organizations designing, developing, or making available high-impact AI systems in Canada
commencement_notes: Introduced June 16, 2022 (44th Parliament, 1st Session). Passed Second Reading in the House of Commons April 24, 2023, referred to the Standing Committee on Industry and Technology (INDU). Parliament prorogued January 6, 2025, causing Bill C-27 and AIDA to die on the order paper. A successor bill had not been re-introduced as of April 2026. Status will be updated if AIDA or an equivalent bill is introduced in a future Parliament.

title: Voluntary Code of Conduct on the Responsible Development and Management of Advanced Generative AI Systems
short_title: Voluntary Code of Conduct on Generative AI
source_name: Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)
summary: A non-binding voluntary commitment framework for organizations developing and deploying advanced generative AI systems in Canada. Launched by ISED in September 2023 with initial signatories including major AI companies. Covers safety evaluations, transparency reporting, watermarking of AI-generated content, and incident reporting. Intended as an interim measure while AIDA was being developed; remains active following AIDA's failure to pass.
scope: Organizations that voluntarily sign on, focused on developers and deployers of advanced generative AI systems (e.g., large language models, image generators).
key_provisions: Safety testing and evaluation before release of advanced generative AI systems
Transparency reports published at least annually, including information on capabilities, limitations, and risk mitigations
Content provenance and watermarking: implement technical means to identify AI-generated content
Incident reporting: notify the government of serious incidents involving generative AI systems
Bias and discrimination testing before deployment
Research collaboration with the government and academic community on AI safety
enforcement_body: Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED) — voluntary compliance, no enforcement mechanism
applies_to: Voluntary signatories developing or deploying advanced generative AI systems in Canada
No mandatory application — organizations opt in
commencement_notes: Launched September 27, 2023. Initial signatories included Cohere, Ada, and other Canadian AI companies, as well as international companies operating in Canada. The code was designed as an interim framework pending AIDA's passage. As of April 2026, it remains the primary government-backed AI governance instrument for generative AI in Canada.

title: Privacy Act
summary: Canada's federal public-sector privacy law governing how federal government institutions collect, use, disclose, retain, and dispose of personal information. Directly applicable to government AI systems that use personal information about Canadians. Works in tandem with the Directive on Automated Decision-Making, which adds AI-specific obligations on top of the Privacy Act's general framework.
scope: All federal government institutions listed in Schedule I of the Act (departments, agencies, Crown corporations).
key_provisions: Institutions may only collect personal information directly related to and necessary for an operating program or activity
Personal information must only be used for the purpose it was collected, or with consent, or as specifically authorized by law
Individuals have the right to access their personal information held by the federal government
Individuals may request correction of inaccurate personal information
Retention and disposal schedules: personal information no longer needed must be destroyed in accordance with approved schedules
Privacy impact assessments required for programs involving significant privacy risks
enforcement_body: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC)
applies_to: Federal government institutions listed in Schedule I of the Privacy Act
Government AI systems processing personal information about individuals
commencement_notes: In force since July 1, 1983. Amended in 2022 (Bill S-7 / Strengthening Privacy for Canadians Act). The government has signalled intent to modernize the Privacy Act to address AI and big data, but no comprehensive reform bill had been introduced as of April 2026.
